---
title: "Theory and Research Design"
authors:
  - name: William Thengs Egeli
    url: https://www.linkedin.com/in/william-thengs-egeli-4204b8356
  - name: Awaatef Hassan
    url: https://www.linkedin.com/in/awaatef-abdullah-hassan-6b1386345
  - name: Zainab Ali
format: html
bibliography: references.bib
csl: apa.csl
---

(IKKE FERDIG)

## Background  and Purpose

The purpose of the survey is to examine whether individuals' attitudes toward a political issue are influenced by the political party it is associated with. In other words: are opinions about policy proposals affected by who presents the proposal, when the content of the proposal is otherwise identical?

## Theoretical Perspective


From a psychological perspective, people often rely on their existing thoughts and views when forming opinions. In some cases, this happens automatically, but in others we consciously consider which arguments are relevant. Framing influences which thoughts are available, considered relevant and easy to recall. The stronger or more relevant a frame appears, the more likely it is to affect our opinions [@chong2007framing].

In this scenario, the respondents might automatically choose their answer based on the party that is mentioned. This emphasizes that we often tend to use thoughts that are available and easy to recall. (!!SETNING OM MOTIVATED REASONING!!). Sometimes this happens automatically, but at the end of the day, framing influences which thoughts seem most relevant.

## Research Design

To answer this research question, we conducted an experiment. Since we wanted to determine whether the party promoting a proposal influences respondents' attitudes toward it, we needed to select an issue on which both parties' actual positions are aligned. It was also important that a considerable (political) distance existed between the two parties. This gives us a better basis for assessing whether the results that follow are actually due to the proposal's association with the party.

The political issue chosen was inheritance tax, and the Labour Party (Arbeiderpartiet) and the Progress Party (Fremskrittspartiet) were selected as the two political parties. Inheritance tax has proven to be a latent political issue — including among our target group, young people. We considered these choices well-suited, partly because they are a) engaging enough to motivate respondents to participate in the survey, and b) an issue on which the Labour Party and the Progress Party are not necessarily perceived as being in agreement at the outset. Furthermore, the Labour Party and the Progress Party are in reality in agreement on the proposal, and therefore also satisfy this requirement.

### Experimental Treatment

To test our hypotheses, we developed a simple survey design in the form of an experiment. Respondents were sent or presented with a QR code leading to a website, where they were randomly assigned (50% probability each) to identical surveys, differing only in our experimental stimulus. The sole difference is therefore whether the claim about inheritance tax is attributed to the Labour Party or the Progress Party. The treatment stimulus was administered by presenting the claim, followed by the question: 

>*"\[The Labour Party / The Progress Party\] believes that there should be no fee or tax on inheriting large assets. To what extent do you agree or disagree with this statement?"* (scale: 1 = Strongly disagree and 5 = Strongly agree). 

In addition, respondents were asked standard demographic control variables common in political surveys: gender, place of residence, age, social media use, and political interest. Respondents also indicated their preferred political party: *Which political party do you feel closest to?*

## Hypotheses

Based on the theory of source framing and motivated reasoning, we derived four concrete hypotheses:

**H1:** Voters who identify the Labour Party as the party they feel most closely affiliated with will be more favorable toward the proposal when the Labour Party is named as its advocate.

**H2:** Voters who identify the Progress Party as the party they feel most closely affiliated with will be more favorable toward the proposal when the Progress Party is named as its advocate.

**H3:** Voters who place themselves on the right side of the left-right political scale express more negative feelings toward the proposal when the Labour Party is named as its advocate. 

**H4:** Voters who place themselves on the left side of the left-right political scale express more negative feelings toward the proposal when the Progress Party is named as its advocate. 

Furthermore, we also expected the overall support for inheritance tax to be lower for parties on the right, while parties on the left show more support for the policy. The Labour Party should therefore in general, according to our prediction, be less favorable toward the proposal than voters of the Progress Party. This expectation holds even though both parties currently share this position, as voters on the left are typically more supportive of redistributive taxation, whereas voters on the right tend to favor lower taxation.


## References
